A **community** ([Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): *cymuned*) is a division of land that forms the lowest tier of [local government in Wales](/wiki/Local_government_in_Wales "Local government in Wales"). Welsh communities are analogous to [civil parishes in England](/wiki/Civil_parishes_in_England "Civil parishes in England") but, unlike English parishes, communities cover the whole of Wales.[[1]](#cite_note-ONS_Geography_Guide-1) There are 878 communities in Wales, with more than 730 having community and town councils.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Community_(Wales)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Until 1974, Wales was divided into [civil parishes](/wiki/Civil_parish "Civil parish").[[3]](#cite_note-ons-3) These were abolished by section 20 (6) of the [Local Government Act 1972](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972"), and replaced by communities by section 27 of the same Act. The [principal areas of Wales](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_Wales#Principal_areas "Subdivisions of Wales") are divided entirely into communities. Unlike in England, where [unparished areas](/wiki/Unparished_area "Unparished area") exist, no part of Wales is outside a community, even in [urban areas](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area").[[3]](#cite_note-ons-3)

Most, but not all, communities are administered by [community councils](/wiki/Community_council "Community council"), which are equivalent to English [parish councils](/wiki/Parish_councils_in_England "Parish councils in England") in terms of their powers and the way they operate. Welsh community councils may call themselves [town councils](/wiki/Town_council "Town council") unilaterally and may have [city status](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of cities in the United Kingdom") granted by [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown"). In Wales, all town councils are community councils. There are now three communities with city status: [Bangor](/wiki/Bangor,_Gwynedd "Bangor, Gwynedd"), [St Asaph](/wiki/St_Asaph "St Asaph") and [St Davids](/wiki/St_Davids "St Davids"). The chair of a town council or city council will usually have the title [mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") (Welsh: *maer*). However, not every community has a council. In communities with populations too small to sustain a full community council, community meetings may be established. The communities in the urban areas of the cities of [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff"), [Swansea](/wiki/Swansea "Swansea") and [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales") do not have community councils.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

At the [2001 United Kingdom census](/wiki/2001_United_Kingdom_census "2001 United Kingdom census"), there were 869 communities in Wales. 84 percent, or more than 730, have a council.[[3]](#cite_note-ons-3) They vary in size from [Rhayader](/wiki/Rhayader "Rhayader") with an area of 13,945 hectares (34,460 acres) to [Cefn Fforest](/wiki/Cefn_Fforest "Cefn Fforest") with an area of 64 hectares (160 acres). They ranged in population, from [Barry](/wiki/Barry,_Vale_of_Glamorgan "Barry, Vale of Glamorgan") with 45,053 recorded inhabitants to [Baglan Bay](/wiki/Baglan_Bay "Baglan Bay") with no permanent residents.

As of 2025, there are 878 communities, although the number of them having community/town councils have remained the same at 730.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

The twenty-two [principal area councils](/wiki/Principal_areas_of_Wales "Principal areas of Wales") are required to review the community boundaries within their area every fifteen years.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The councils propose changes to the [Local Democracy and Boundary Commission for Wales](/wiki/Local_Democracy_and_Boundary_Commission_for_Wales "Local Democracy and Boundary Commission for Wales"), which prepares a report and makes recommendations to the [Welsh Government](/wiki/Welsh_Government "Welsh Government"). If the Welsh Government accepts the recommendations, then it implements them using a [statutory instrument](/wiki/Statutory_instrument_(UK) "Statutory instrument (UK)").[[8]](#cite_note-8) For example, in 2016 four new communities were created in the [City and County of Cardiff](/wiki/City_and_County_of_Cardiff "City and County of Cardiff").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The legislation surrounding community councils in Wales has been amended significantly in the [Local Government (Wales) Act 1994](/wiki/Local_Government_(Wales)_Act_1994 "Local Government (Wales) Act 1994") and the [Local Government (Wales) Measure 2011](/w/index.php?title=Local_Government_(Wales)_Measure_2011&action=edit&redlink=1 "Local Government (Wales) Measure 2011 (page does not exist)").[[10]](#cite_note-10)
